coredata.dc:title: Constructing a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst I-ABN-C3N4@CDs/BiOI by synergistically in situ modified g-C3N4 via carbon dots and copolymerization for BPA degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Junge
Ding, Yuan
Ma, Xianggang
Liu, Yingliang
Zhang, Li
Wang, Kunlun
Cao, Shaokui
Xu, Shengang
coredata.dc:description: The rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and the narrow absorption range of visible light greatly limit the photocatalytic activity of graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4). Herein, g-C3N4 was jointly modified by carbon dots (CDs) and copolymerization via in situ co-calcining of three precursors (urea, glucose and 2-amino-5-iodo-benzonitrile (I-ABN)), and named as I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy. Then, solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts PC1–PC9 with CDs as the electron mediators were prepared via in situ growth of BiOI on the surface of I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy through solvothermal method. The optimal proportion of each component was explored through orthogonal experiments. The existence of CDs act as an electron transport medium to accelerate the transportation rate of photogenerated carriers, improve the light absorption capacity. The copolymerization with I-ABN introduce benzene rings and I− to g-C3N4, which enhance the light absorption capacity and photoelectric efficiency. The Z-scheme heterojunction effectively suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Therefore, PC1–PC9 presented excellent performance for the degradation of BPA due to the synergistic effects of the above three points. Among them, PC6 showed the best photocatalytic activity, which completely degraded BPA (15 mg/L) within 40 min, and whose rate constant (0.091 min−1) was 22.8 times that of bulk g-C3N4. The active radical trapping experiment determined •O2 − and h+ were the main active radicals. Finally, the BPA degradation mechanism by PC6 was proposed. This study provides new insights for the combination of heterojunction, carbon dots, and copolymerization to rationally design efficient photocatalysts to restore the environment.

head.ce:title: Constructing a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst I-ABN-C3N4@CDs/BiOI by synergistically in situ modified g-C3N4 via carbon dots and copolymerization for BPA degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and the narrow absorption range of visible light greatly limit the photocatalytic activity of graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4). Herein, g-C3N4 was jointly modified by carbon dots (CDs) and copolymerization via in situ co-calcining of three precursors (urea, glucose and 2-amino-5-iodo-benzonitrile (I-ABN)), and named as I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy. Then, solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts PC1–PC9 with CDs as the electron mediators were prepared via in situ growth of BiOI on the surface of I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy through solvothermal method. The optimal proportion of each component was explored through orthogonal experiments. The existence of CDs act as an electron transport medium to accelerate the transportation rate of photogenerated carriers, improve the light absorption capacity. The copolymerization with I-ABN introduce benzene rings and I− to g-C3N4, which enhance the light absorption capacity and photoelectric efficiency. The Z-scheme heterojunction effectively suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Therefore, PC1–PC9 presented excellent performance for the degradation of BPA due to the synergistic effects of the above three points. Among them, PC6 showed the best photocatalytic activity, which completely degraded BPA (15 mg/L) within 40 min, and whose rate constant (0.091 min−1) was 22.8 times that of bulk g-C3N4. The active radical trapping experiment determined •O2 − and h+ were the main active radicals. Finally, the BPA degradation mechanism by PC6 was proposed. This study provides new insights for the combination of heterojunction, carbon dots, and copolymerization to rationally design efficient photocatalysts to restore the environment.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, energy shortage and environmental pollution have become two important issues of concern to us. In particular, various organic wastewater and pollutants have caused irreversible damage to the environment and human beings. For instance, bisphenol A (BPA), is one of the most widely used industrial compounds in the world. It is mainly used in the production of polycarbonates, epoxy resins, unsaturated polyester resins, and other polymer materials [1,2]. When BPA is released into the environment, people are committed to exploring low-cost, high-efficient, and environmentally friendly methods to remove it. Nowadays, the common degradation methods of BPA include biodegradation [3,4], electrocatalysis [5], adsorption [6], Fenton process [7], photocatalytic oxidation [8,9], and so on. Among them, photocatalytic oxidation has been widely used because of its simplicity, high efficiency, low energy consumption, and environmental friendliness [10,11].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as an organic metal-free semiconductor catalyst, has attracted much attention because of its narrow energy band gap (2.7 eV), excellent electronic and optical properties, nontoxicity, and easy preparation [12,13]. However, the shortcomings of g-C3N4 such as high photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate, low conductivity, narrow light absorption range, and small specific surface area limit its application. At present, various methods have been used to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, such as copolymerization modification [14,15], morphology regulation [16,17], element doping [18], heterojunction engineering [19,20] and composite modification with carbon materials [21]. It is a promising material for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants.

As a new zero-dimensional carbon material, carbon dots (CDs) have good optical absorption properties and electrical conductivity. The combination of CDs and semiconductor photocatalysts can broaden the optical absorption range of the material, promote the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers inside the photocatalyst, and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [22–24]. In our previous work [25], we prepared a CDs modified g-C3N4 photocatalyst by a one-step double in situ method for the splitting of water into hydrogen under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic performance of the CDs@g-C3N4 photocatalyst was 4.5 times that of bulk g-C3N4. Compared to the conventional two-step composite method, except that the preparation process of the one-step double in situ calcination method is simpler, CDs could bind more closely to g-C3N4 because the precursors of CDs and g-C3N4 are contacted at the molecular level [26].

Copolymerization is also considered a typical strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. The copolymerization of small molecular monomers containing aromatic rings and g-C3N4 precursors can integrate the aromatic ring of the comonomer into the g-C3N4 network through multiple chemical reactions [14,27]. Aromatic rings can enhance their π electron delocalization, broaden the light absorption range and improve the conductivity of materials. In our previous work [25], 2-amino-5-iodo-benzonitriles (I-ABN) modified g-C3N4 was prepared by in situ calcination of the mixture of I-ABN and dicyandiamide. Under visible light irradiation, the performance of this material in splitting water into hydrogen was about 17.7 times that of bulk g-C3N4. However, up to now, the application of comonomer-modified g-C3N4 in the degradation of pollutions has been rarely reported.

Bismuth oxyiodine (BiOI) is a new two-dimensional layered semiconductor material with excellent photocatalysis. In the crystal structure of BiOI, the internal electric field formed by the asymmetric charge distribution between [Bi2O2]2+ and [I2]2− is conducive to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, so as to improve the photocatalytic activity [28]. Considering the unique properties of BiOI, it is a very attractive strategy to construct heterojunction composites by coupling g-C3N4 and BiOI. Heterojunction engineering, especially all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunctions, can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs in semiconductor photocatalysts and improve their photocatalytic efficiency [29,30]. Although there are relevant reports on BiOI/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts, the photocatalytic performance are still low due to the low migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and insufficient absorption of visible light. Inspired by the superiority of CDs and copolymerization in improving the optical absorption and accelerating photogenerated carrier transport in composites. Synergistic modification of g-C3N4 through the introduction of CDs, copolymerization and construction of heterojunctions is expected to be an effective strategy. The obtained composites are expected to exhibit higher photocatalytic properties than the BiOI/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts. The reason may be as follows: The construction of Z-scheme heterojunctions provides a channel for carrier transport, effectively avoiding the recombination of electron–hole pairs. CDs can act as an electron transport medium to accelerate the transportation rate of photogenerated carriers, enhance the light absorption range [24]. The introduction of benzene rings and I− through the copolymerization with I-ABN can further enhance the light absorption capacity and promote the separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [25].

Based on the above considerations, in this work, a synergistically modified g-C3N4, I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy, was firstly prepared by calcinating the three precursors, urea, glucose, and I-ABN simultaneously. After calcination, except for the formation of g-C3N4, CDs transformed from glucose will be introduced into g-C3N4. In addition, benzene rings and iodine ions (I−) will also be introduced into g-C3N4 due to the copolymerization of I-ABN. Then, BiOI was deposited on the surface of I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy to construct a novel Z-scheme photocatalyst (PC1–PC9) with CDs as the solid-state electron mediators. The two in situ methods (in situ calcination and in situ deposition) will make the Z-scheme components keep intimate contact. The photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared materials were explored by degradation of BPA under visible light irradiation. PC6 showed the best photocatalytic activity, which completely degraded BPA (15 mg/L) within 40 min. Based on the analytical characterization and photochemical experiments, a possible degradation mechanism was proposed.

Glucose (AR), bismuth (Ⅲ) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, ≥99.5 %), and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. 2-amino-5-iodo-benzonitriles (I-ABN) were purchased from Shaoyuan Chemical Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Potassium iodide (KI), and bisphenol A (BPA, ≥99.5 %) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Shanghai, China. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

The CDs modified g-C3N4 were prepared by in situ approach using urea and glucose as precursors. Typically, 6 g of urea was fully dissolved in a crucible containing 12.5 mL of deionized water, and 1 mL of glucose aqueous solution in different concentrations (2, 3, and 4 mg/L) was added. Then the crucible was placed in an oven at 80 °C to make most of the water was evaporated. Finally, the crucible was transferred to a tubular furnace, heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and calcined for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained materials were named as g-C3N4@CDsy (y = 2, 3, and 4), here y represents the milligrams of glucose added to urea. For comparison, bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by the same method and named BCN.

I-ABNx-CN@CDsy (x = 2.5, 5, 7.5; y = 2, 3, 4, where x, y represents the milligrams of I-ABN and glucose added to urea, respectively) samples were prepared by heating I-ABN, glucose, and urea together following the literature [25]. Take the preparation process of I-ABN2.5-CN@CDs3 as an example: 6 g of urea was fully dissolved in a crucible containing 12.5 mL of deionized water; Then 1 mL of glucose aqueous solution (3 mg/L) and 2.5 mg of I-ABN were added to the crucible, respectively. The mixture was heated to 80 °C under stirring and kept at this temperature until all water was evaporated. Lastly, the crucible was transferred to a tubular furnace, heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and calcined at this temperature for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. The composite materials were obtained and named I-ABN2.5-CN@CDs3.

Taking PC6 as an example, the preparation procedure is as follows: Firstly, I-ABN2.5-C3N4/CDs3 was prepared by co-calcinating 6 g of urea, 2.5 mg of I-ABN, and 3 mg of glucose with the procedure described in 2.2.2. Secondly, 39.29 mg of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 200 mg I-ABN2.5-C3N4/CDs3 were added into 20 mL of ethylene glycol, and stirred evenly for 4 h to obtain solution A. Then 13.44 mg of KI was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water to obtain solution B. Subsequently, solution B was added to solution A, and stirred for 30 min. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature and the resulting orange-yellow powder was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The above synthesized photocatalyst was named PC6. Using the same procedure as that of PC6, all the other eight photocatalysts were prepared.

The surface morphology and microtopography of the as-prepared samples were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, PhenomLE, Netherlands) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1400v-Flash, Japan), respectively. The crystal phase composition of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku/SmartLab SE, Japan) with a Cu-Kα (λ = 1.540598 Å), while the XRD data were collected in a 2θ range from 10° to 80°. The chemical bond properties and molecular structure were investigated by a Bruker Tensor II FT-IR apparatus using KBr as a diluent with a wavenumber range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The chemical composition of the samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the spectra were recorded on an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples were determined on a UV–visible diffuse reflection spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 5000), using BaSO4 as the reference.

Sample powders were placed in a sample bath and tested at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Photoluminescence emission spectra of the samples were collected using a fluorescence spectrometer (QuantaMaster 40) in the wavelength range of 400–650 nm.

The transient photocurrent response test was accomplished on a standard three-electrode electrochemical analyzer (CHI-660E, Shanghai Chenhua PR China). The ITO glass coated with as-prepared samples, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) and platinum wire were used as working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. The Na2SO4 (0.1 M) solution and 300 W Xe lamp equipped with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm) were employed as electrolyte and light sources of the system, respectively.

The photocatalytic activity of all catalyst samples were evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of BPA under visible light irradiation conditions. A 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 420 nm UV-light cut-off filter was used as the light source. As for the photodegradation experiment, 50 mg of the as-prepared catalyst was dispersed in a solution containing 100 mL of BPA aqueous solution (15 mg/L) and stirred under dark conditions for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium before irradiation. The stirring was continued during irradiation to keep the mixed solution suspended. Subsequently, 4 mL of suspension was collected at 10 min intervals and the catalyst was separated by 0.22 μm membrane filtration. The concentration of BPA in the obtained solution was determined by UV–visible spectroscopy at λ = 276 nm. The degradation efficiency of BPA was calculated by the following formula:
(1)
η =
C t
−
C 0
C 0
×
100 %

Where C 0 and Ct are the concentrations of BPA before and after degradation, η refers to the photodegradation efficiency. In addition, to investigate the recyclable stability of the catalyst, cycling experiments were performed under the same reaction conditions. After 60 min of the reaction, the photocatalysts were recovered by centrifugation. The collected samples were washed with water and dried at 60 °C. The next degradation experiment was carried out after the catalyst was supplemented.

The detailed morphology and microstructure of the prepared samples were observed with TEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, BCN possesses a two-dimensional sheet stacked structure, which is a typical characteristic morphology of g-C3N4 [31]. Fig. 1b shows that g-C3N4@CDs3 also presents an irregular block structure with multiple layers stacked, demonstrating that the introduction of CDs do not affect the morphology of g-C3N4 [31]. And in Fig. 1c, it can also be seen that CDs with a size of 8–10 nm are closely connected with g-C3N4. Compared with g-C3N4@CDs3, the morphology of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 (Fig. 1d) shows a distinct sheet structure and a more regular shape. The change may be due to the introduction of the benzene rings and I−will affect the crystallization process of g-C3N4 and then make the morphology of the material more regular [25]. Fig. 1e exhibits the TEM image of PC6, showing that BiOI has successfully grown on the surface of g-C3N4. At the same time, CDs with a size of about 10 nm also can be observed, which also demonstrated the successful introduction of CDs. And in Fig. 1f, the lattice spacing of 0.33 nm and 0.28 nm correspond to the (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4 and the (110) crystal of BiOI, respectively, which can also prove the successful composite of BiOI and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 [32]. And the elemental spectra of C, N, Bi, O, and I show clear definitions and uniform distribution of these elements. Except for the expected five elements, there are no other impurity elements. In addition, the shape and distribution of the mapping images of Bi, I, and O are basically the same, which also confirms the presence of BiOI in the sample. Moreover, the mapping images of C and N show a wider distribution as compared to that of BiOI, thus confirming the growth of BiOI on the g-C3N4 surface [33]. The above results demonstrate the formation of good interfacial contact between BiOI and g-C3N4.

The phase structure of the prepared samples was identified by XRD. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, BCN exhibits two peaks at 27.5° and 13.1°, which can be assigned to (002) and (100) diffraction planes of g-C3N4, respectively. While the strong peak at 27.5 °corresponds to the planar stacking of conjugated CN heterocycles, and the weak peak at 13.1° is ascribed to the in-plane repeat units of tri-s-triazine [34]. The XRD profile of g-C3N4@CDs3 and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 all show the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4, indicating that the crystal structure of g-C3N4 was not disrupted after the introduction of CDs and the copolymerization with I-ABN [35]. As for PC6, except for the two characteristic peaks of g-C3N4, the peaks at 29.7°, 31.7°, 45.4°, and 55.3° correspond to the (102), (110), (200), and (212) crystal surfaces of the BiOI [36], respectively. The coexistence of characteristic diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and BiOI are observed in PC6, indicating the successful preparation of the composite photocatalyst, which is consistent with the TEM characterization results. To further investigate the crystal structure of the composite, the XRD patterns of PC1–PC9 were compared. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak patterns of them are generally identical. The difference is when the BiOI mass fraction increases from PC1 to PC3, the intensity of the BiOI diffraction peak in the composite becomes stronger [37]. No obvious CDs characteristic peak was found in the XRD spectra of the photocatalysts, which may be due to the low content of CDs on the surface, which can't be detected by the instrument [38].

The functional group composition and structure of the material were investigated by FT-IR spectra. The FT-IR spectra of PC6 was compared with that of the BCN, BiOI, g-C3N4@CDs3, and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, the FT-IR spectra of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and g-C3N4@CDs3 are almost the same as that of g-C3N4. The peaks in the range of 1200–1600 cm−1 could be assigned to the typical stretching vibration of CN and CN [39]. The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 is ascribed to the characteristic vibration of triazine rings [40]. And the absorption peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1 indicate the presence of amino and surface hydroxyl groups [41]. Besides, the peak of carbon-iodine bonds in the spectra of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 is not observed, which may be due to the low content of I atoms in the g-C3N4 structure. PC6 also exhibits the typical stretching vibration of g-C3N4, and the peak located at 507 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic stretching mode of Bi–O [42]. Additionally, the peak of PC6 becomes wider at 3000–3300 cm−1, probably caused by the bending vibration of the H2O and COOH adsorbed on the surface of BiOI [43]. The peaks of FT-IR spectra of PC1–PC9 are consistent, all of them represent the typical stretching vibration peak of g-C3N4 and BiOI. But the stretching vibration peak of BiOI is weak, which may be due to the low content of BiOI in the composite, resulting in the low intensity of the stretching vibration peak.

The element composition and chemical state of PC6 were further elucidated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 4
a showed the spectrum of PC6. The catalyst contains five elements: C, N, Bi, O, and I, which is consistent with its chemical composition. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 4b) consist of four peaks located at 284.8, 286.6, 288.1, and 289.1 eV, which correspond to the CC, CNH2, NCN, and -COOH, respectively [44]. This result indicates the presence of CDs in the composite, which is consistent with its TEM characterization. The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 4c) can be decomposed into four binding energies corresponding to the C=NC bond (398.3 eV), the N-(C)3 group (399.7 eV), and the CNH2 functional group (400.8 eV), respectively [45]. Moreover, the weak peak centered at 403.8 eV can be attributed to the charge effect of the heptazine ring. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. 4d), two strong peaks are observed at 158.3 and 163.6 eV, relating to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 orbitals of Bi3+, respectively [46]. The O1s peak (Fig. 4e) are located at 529.2, 531.2, and 533.3 eV, which correspond to the BiO bonds in the BiOI, the H2O and -OH bonds adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst, respectively [47]. In the spectrum of I (Fig. 4f), the peak observed at 618.1 eV corresponding to I 3d5/2 is related to I−, demonstrating that the iodide ion is successfully doped in situ into the network of g-C3N4 [48]. And the peak located at 629.6 eV corresponding to I 3d3/2 which may be related to the C-I bond formed between BiOI and g-C3N4 [49]. The characterization results of the XPS demonstrated that PC6 consists of g-C3N4, CDs, I-ABN, and BiOI, which was consistent with the TEM, XRD and FT-IR results.

The optical properties of the material play a crucial role in its photocatalytic activity. The absorption optical properties of the prepared samples were clarified by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 5 a, g-C3N4@CDs3 exhibits an enormous redshift compared with BCN, this phenomenon may be attributed to the upconverting PL properties of CDs and the action of photosensitizers [50]. While compared with g-C3N4@CDs3, I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 displays a further redshift, indicating that the simultaneous introduction of aromatic rings and halogen doping into g-C3N4 can enhance the light-harvesting ability of g-C3N4 in the UV–visible region. Moreover, BiOI shows the most extensive optical absorption, with an absorption edge of about 650 nm. After I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 compositing with BiOI, it shows an obvious red shift and high intensity, implying that the heterojunction structure between I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and BiOI is successfully constructed [46]. As presented in Fig. S1, all PC1–PC9 represent evident absorption in the range of 200–800 nm and have strong absorption performance in the range of 200–600 nm. The band gap energy of the as-prepared materials can be calculated by the following formula [51]:
(2)
α h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)
n / 2
where α, hν, A, and Eg represent the absorption coefficient, light energy, proportional constant, and band gap energy, respectively. The value of n represents the property of the transition (n = 1 is the direct transition, and n = 4 is the indirect transition) [46]. According to the relevant literature, g-C3N4 and BiOI belong to direct bandgap semiconductor and indirect bandgap semiconductor, respectively [46], so the values of n are 1 and 4. The band gap of BCN, BiOI, g-C3N4@CDs3, and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 were estimated to be 2.88, 1.89, 2.63, and 2.51 eV. After introducing CDs and benzene rings into g-C3N4, the band gap of g-C3N4 is reduced from 2.88 eV to 2.51 eV. Therefore, the introduction of benzene rings, I ions and CDs tends to expand the range of semiconductor light response, thus facilitating the absorption and utilization of more visible light and are beneficial to improving the photocatalytic properties of the material. Furthermore, the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) can be determined according to the following approach [52]:
(3)

where X represents the electronegativity of the material, EVB and ECB represent the potential of VB and CB, and EC is the free energy corresponding to the hydrogen scale of about 4.5 eV [53]. The value of X for g-C3N4 and BiOI correspond to 4.67 and 5.94 [54], respectively. According to Eqs. (3) and (4), the CB and VB potentials of the I-ABN photocatalyst are −1.10 V and 1.14 V, and that of BiOI are 0.5 V and 2.39 V, respectively.

It is well-known that Mott−Schottky curve examination is an effective electrochemical technique to determine the semiconductor type. The curve of n-type semiconductors have a positive slope, and p-type semiconductors have a negative slope [55]. Fig. 5c are the Mott−Schottky curves of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and BiOI. The I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 is evidently n-type. And the curve of BiOI also presents a positive slope that implies its n-type nature. And the flat band potential of a semiconductor can be estimated by extrapolating the Mott–Schottky straight line to the intercept on the horizontal axis, which is also considered to be the Fermi level of the semiconductor [56].

The electron–hole separation efficiency is one of the primary factors affecting the photocatalytic ability of photocatalysts. The separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be investigated by PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the PL intensity of g-C3N4@CDs3 is significantly reduced compared with BCN, possibly because CDs can facilitate the migration of electrons produced by g-C3N4 to CDs and thus reduce the recombination rate of electron–hole pairs. The PL intensity of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 is further reduced, indicating that the introduction of benzene rings and I ions via the copolymerization of I-ABN have significantly inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Besides, PC6 exhibits the lowest PL intensity, which is mainly caused by the Z-scheme heterojunction formed between I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and BiOI. The internal electric field established between I-ABN2.5-C3N4 and BiOI accelerates the separation of electron–hole pairs and effectively inhibits the recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, thereby improving photocatalytic activity. Fig. 6b showed the PL spectra of PC1–PC9. Among them, PC6 has the lowest PL intensity, indicating that the photogenerated electron–hole pairs recombination rate is significantly suppressed.

The separation and transfer behavior of photogenerated charges were further investigated by transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As displayed in Fig. 7 a, compared with BCN, the transient photocurrent response of g-C3N4@CDs3 and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 have improved significantly, indicating that the introduction of CDs, benzene rings and I ions can effectively promote the transportation of photogenerated carriers in g-C3N4 [25,57]. After compositing I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 with BiOI, the transient photocurrent response of PC6 is further improved, suggesting that the heterojunction structure can provide a transportation channel for photogenerated carriers and improve its photogenerated electron migration efficiency. The electrochemical impedance spectrum (Fig. 7b) showed the interfacial charge transfer properties of the samples, where the smaller semicircular radius corresponds to a better charge transfer capability [25]. The increasing order of the arc radius is as follows: PC6<g-C3N4@CDs3/BiOI<I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3<g-C3N4@CDs3<BCN. Both I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and g-C3N4@CDs3 show a smaller arc radius than g-C3N4, which proves CDs, benzene rings and I ions can effectively improve the separation of electron hole pairs. PC6 exhibits the minimum radius of curvature, indicating that the Z scheme heterojunction structure can reduce the migration resistance of its photogenerated carriers, and improve the transportation rate of photogenerated carriers. And it is consistent with the results of PL, transient photocurrent response and the subsequent degradation efficiency of BPA. The photoelectrochemical test results exhibit that the synergetic effect of CDs, copolymerization and Z-scheme heterojunction can effectively improve the separation rate of the photogenerated charge of the photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic properties of all photocatalysts were explored by the degradation of BPA under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8 a and c, the degradation efficiency of g-C3N4@CDs3 was much higher than that of BCN, and its degradation rate constant is 2.5 times that of BCN, indicating that the addition of a little glucose (about 0.05 wt% of urea) could largely increase the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. The reason may be that glucose could be transformed into CDs [58]. And CDs could effectively enhance the light absorption range, reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increase the transporting ability of carriers, which were confirmed by UV–vis DRS spectra ( Fig. 5a), PL spectra (Fig. 6a) and photocurrent response (Fig. 7a), respectively. As for I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3, its rate constant is 1.6 times that of g-C3N4@CDs3, demonstrating that the copolymerization of I-ABN (about 0.042 wt% of urea) and CDs could synergically improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. The copolymerization of I-ABN could introduce aromatic rings and I− to g-C3N4 [25], resulting in the improvement of light absorption (Fig. 5a), the reduction of the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs (Fig. 6a), and the increasement of the transporting ability of carriers, respectively. After the combination of g-C3N4 with BiOI, the degradation rate of BPA by g-C3N4/BiOI10 could only reach about 46 % within 60 min under illumination. This further suggests that I-ABN and CDs play an important role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. After g-C3N4@CDs3 and I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 were composited with BiOI to construct heterojunction photocatalysts g-C3N4@CDs3/BiOI and PC6, respectively, their photocatalytic activities were dramatically improved again. The rate constants of g-C3N4@CDs3/BiOI10% and PC6 are 17.3 and 22.8 times that of BCN, which may be caused by the formation of solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and BiOI using CDs as the electron mediators, and the Z-scheme heterojunction provides a channel for photogenerated carrier transfer, effectively avoiding the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. This result also further confirmed that the co-calcination of three precursors is an effective means to modify g-C3N4. And under visible light irradiation, the mixture of I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and BiOI can only degrade about 40 % of BPA in 1 h, and its degradation performance is far inferior to PC6, indicating that the simple physical mixture cannot effectively improve the photocatalytic degradation performance of g-C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 8b and d, PC1-PC9 all show superior degradation activity of BPA under illumination for 60 min. The results are consistent with their PL, photocurrent response, and EIS results. Among them, PC6 showed the best degradation efficiency and the highest corresponding degradation rate constant. PC6 could degrade BPA by about 98 % under illumination for 40 min. Fig. 8c depicts the pseudo-first-order reaction model for the degradation of BPA at different photocatalysts, and the corresponding fitted data are presented in Figs. 8d and S3. The fitting results indicate that the photocatalytic degradation of BPA by the catalyst conforms well to the model of −ln(C/C 0) = kt, in which C0 is the initial concentration of BPA, C is the concentration of BPA at t h, and k is the reaction rate constant [36]. From Fig. 8d, it can be seen that the rate constant of PC6 is up to 0.091 min−1, which is 22.8, 7.6, 9.1, and 5.7 times that of BCN, BiOI, g-C3N4@CDs3, I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3, respectively. In addition, compared to other catalysts, PC6 also exhibits outstanding photocatalytic degradation efficiency. (Table S1, ESI)

The effects of the amount of photocatalyst, the initial concentration of BPA, and the pH value of the solution on the photocatalytic performance of PC6 were further studied. As displayed in Fig. 9 a, when the catalyst dosage catalyst increased from 10 mg to 50 mg, the photocatalytic activity of PC6 was gradually increased, which may be due to the increasing active species in the reaction system. But when the catalyst dosage catalyst continuously increased to 70 mg, the activity of the photocatalyst decreased to a certain extent, mainly because the excessive photocatalyst in the reaction system will produce a light-shielding effect, which reduced the light absorption performance of the catalyst [59]. The other conditions were kept constant and 50 mg of PC6 was added to the reaction system with initial BPA concentrations of 15, 20, 30, and 40 mg/L (Fig. 9b), respectively. With the increase of pollutant concentration, the photocatalytic degradation activity of PC6 decreased gradually. The reason may be that the number of active species produced by photocatalysts of the same quality is limited, thus the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst will be reduced as the initial concentration of BPA increases [60]. Fig. 9c revealed that PC6 shows the optimal photocatalytic performance for the degradation of BPA at pH = 7.13. Under the weak acidic condition, the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst decreases to some extent, and the performance of the photocatalytic degradation of BPA is further decreased with the increasing acidity of the reaction system because the conduction band potential of the catalyst will move in a positive direction, which is not conducive to the generation of O2 − [61]. When the reaction system is under strong alkali condition, the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst is also gradually reduced. The dissociation constant (pKa) of BPA has a range of 9.6–10.2 [61]. When the pH value of the reaction system exceeds the value of pKa, BPA will generate BPA anion through deprotonation, which reduces the surface interaction of BPA and the photocatalyst [62], thus inhibiting the activity of photocatalytic degradation of BPA. Therefore, the photocatalyst can maintain the best photocatalytic degradation activity of BPA under neutral conditions.

The cycle stability and reusability of photocatalysts are of great significance for the practical application of photocatalysts. Therefore, the five consecutive cycle experiments for the degradation of BPA were performed with the recycled PC6 under the same reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. 9d, after five cycles of reaction, the activity of PC6 did not decrease significantly, demonstrating its high stability and recycling ability. In addition, as shown in Fig. S2, the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra were characterized for PC6 before and after cycling experiments, respectively. As expected, the XRD pattern and FT-IR spectrum of the photocatalyst did not change significantly after five consecutive cycle experiments, suggesting the crystal structure and chemical structure of PC6 were still maintained, further demonstrating its good stability and reusability.

The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of BPA by PC6 under visible light irradiation was investigated by a free radical capture experiment. Isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and triethanolamine (TEOA) were employed as scavengers with the concentration of 0.01 mol/L for •OH, •O2 − and hole (h+)[63], respectively. As displayed in Fig. 10 , IPA had a slight inhibitory effect on the degradation process of BPA (reduced from 98 % to 76 %), while BQ and TEOA inhibited it significantly, which reduced the removal rate of BPA to 42% and 55% compared with no scavenger, respectively. Active free radical experiments elucidate that O2 − and h+ are the main active species. To further confirm the above results, EPR testing was performed on PC6. In Fig. 10b and c, it was observed that the ratio of peak heights for DMPO-∙OH signals is 1:2:2:1 and for DMPO-∙O2 − signals is 1:1:1. This confirms that ∙O2 – and ∙OH are indeed produced in the degradation system of PC6. The EPR analysis results are consistent with the above mentioned free radical trapping experiment results.

Based on the above data analysis, the Z scheme mechanism for charge separation and transfer is proposed. Fig. 11 a is the schematic diagram of the energy band structure of the I-ABN2.5-C3N4@CDs3 and BiOI materials before coupling. The two semiconductor materials have different Fermi energy levels, so when they come into contact, this difference leads to the photogenerated electrons transfer to an equilibrium state, thereby facilitating the establishment of heterojunction structures [36]. So the electrons in I-ABN2.5-C3N4 will spontaneously flow through the carbon dots to BiOI until their Fermi energy reach the same level. As a result, I-ABN2.5-C3N4 is positively charged due to the loss of electrons, causing its band edge to bend upwards, while the accumulation of electrons makes BiOI negatively charged, with the band edge bending in a downward direction. At the same time, an internal electric field is formed from between I-ABN2.5-C3N4 and BiOI (Fig. 11b). Under visible light irradiation, both I-ABN2.5-C3N4 and BiOI are excited to generate electron–hole pairs, and photogenerated electrons transition to their respective CB, leaving holes in the VB. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiOI and the holes in the VB of I-ABN2.5-C3N4 tend to recombine at the interface facilitated by CDs, accelerating the transportation rate of photogenerated carriers. The Z-scheme heterojunction can maintain excellent redox ability and effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of PC6. If the charge transfer follows the type II heterojunction mechanism, the e − on the CB ofI-ABN2.5-C3N4 will transfer to the ECB of BiOI and the h + on the EVB of BiOI will transfer to the EVB of I-ABN2.5-C3N4. Considering that the conduction band potential (0.5 V) of BiOI is more positive than the potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 V), so the e − on the CB of BiOI fails to react with oxygen to generate •O2 −. The VB potential ofI-ABN2.5-C3N4 is 1.41 V, which is more negative than that of •OH (1.99 V), implying that the h + on the CB of I-ABN2.5-C3N4 cannot produce •OH. This is inconsistent with the analysis of our free radical trapping experiments and the results of the EPR tests.

Based on the above analysis, the Z-scheme photocatalytic reaction mechanism on PC6 is described in Fig. 11c. Under visible light irradiation, the internally generated photogenerated electrons of I-ABN2.5-C3N4 and BiOI will transit to CB, leaving h + on VB. The e − on the CB of I-ABN2.5-C3N4 is first transferred to the CDs surface to avoid recombination with the holes in the VB. Later, the e − on the CDs surface will react with O2 in the reaction system to generate •O2 −, which will oxidize BPA into small molecular products, and eventually mineralize the small molecular products into H2O and CO2 [21]. And h + in the VB of I-ABN2.5-C3N4 will recombine with the e − in the CB of BiOI via CDs as a mediator. As for BiOI, on the one hand, h + in VB of BiOI can directly oxidize BPA, and degrade it into small molecular substances, H2O and CO2; On the other hand, h + can react with H2O to generate •OH, which further reacts with BPA to open the ring of the benzene ring and mineralize it into H2O and CO2 [64].

In summary, the I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy material was prepared by in situ co-calcining three precursors (urea, I-ABN, and glucose). Then, Z-scheme photocatalysts PC1–PC9 with CDs as the electron transport medium were prepared by in situ growth of BiOI on the surface of I-ABNx-C3N4@CDsy through solvothermal method. Firstly, CDs formed from glucose could enhance the light absorption range and reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs; Secondly, the aromatic rings and I− could be introduced into the g-C3N4 structure through the copolymerization of I-ABN to improve its optical absorption performance and accelerate its transportation rate of photogenerated carriers; Thirdly, the Z-scheme heterojunction provides a channel for the transportation of photogenerated carriers, effectively avoiding the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs of g-C3N4 or BiOI. The synergistic effects of the above three points promote the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of BPA, which can completely degrade BPA (15 mg/L) within about 40 min. After 5 cycles of experiments, PC6 still maintained excellent degradation activity. The active radical trapping experiment showed that •O2 − and h + are the main active radicals in the photocatalyst degradation process of BPA. This research provides new opportunities for the combination of heterojunction, carbon dots, and copolymerization to design efficient photocatalysts to restore the environment.
